functions.description: The Buddy mascot's mood with its presentation logic: a case-insensitive initializer from a raw coach string that falls back to ready, plus an accent color and a caption per mood used by the mascot halo and mood chip.
functions.name: BuddyMood

functions.description: Hero card pairing Buddy and its mood chip with the step-goal ring.
functions.name: BuddySummaryCard

functions.description: The friendly dog mascot drawn with SwiftUI shapes; eyes, tongue, and accessory change with BuddyMood. Exercised visually by the Today dashboard application scenarios.
functions.name: BuddyView

functions.description: The AI coach recommendation card, with a calm brand treatment normally and an amber shield-marked treatment when the recommendation carries a safety flag.
functions.name: CoachCard

functions.description: The day-one empty state shown before Apple Health is connected: Buddy waving, a warm explainer, the connect call-to-action, and a privacy reassurance.
functions.name: ConnectHero

functions.description: Root of the RunBuddy app. Reads the seeded TodayState at launch and shows either the day-one Connect hero or the full Today dashboard.
functions.name: ContentView

functions.description: Small pill under Buddy naming the current mood in its accent color.
functions.name: MoodChip

functions.description: Observable model for the Today dashboard. Reads the day's TodayState from flat rb-prefixed UserDefaults keys seeded at launch, and exposes derived values: goalProgress clamped to 1.0, stepsRemaining, goalReached, and a connect action that flips the day-one hero into the dashboard.
functions.name: RunBuddyModel

functions.description: A single labeled metric tile with an icon, value, and caption.
functions.name: StatTile

functions.description: The three at-a-glance metric tiles: active minutes, distance, and time since moving.
functions.name: StatsRow

functions.description: Circular progress ring toward the daily step goal with the count and a to-go or goal-hit caption.
functions.name: StepRing

functions.description: The full Today surface composed from the header, Buddy summary, stats row, and the coach, latest-activity, and weekly-load cards. Exercised by the Today dashboard application scenarios.
functions.name: TodayDashboard

functions.description: The dashboard's top header with the Today title, the day, and the RunBuddy wordmark.
functions.name: TodayHeader

functions.description: Colored pill describing the week's training-load trend, with distinct colors for spiking, recovering, building, and steady.
functions.name: TrendBadge

functions.description: The week's training-load snapshot: mileage, run days, longest run, and rest days, with a trend badge.
functions.name: WeeklyLoadCard

functions.description: Summary of the most recent run, walk, or ride: distance and pace headline plus duration, date, and source.
functions.name: WorkoutCard

functions.description: Formats a whole number with locale thousands separators for readable step counts, e.g. 11240 becomes 11,240.
functions.name: formatted

functions.description: Renders minutes-since-last-movement as a compact label: now at or below zero, bare minutes under an hour, and hours-plus-minutes above, dropping the minutes part when it is zero.
functions.name: movementLabel

functions.description: Converts an ISO yyyy-MM-dd date into a short weekday-and-day label such as Mon, Jun 22, returning the input unchanged when it is not a valid ISO date.
functions.name: prettyDate

userPrompt:
Project context: Yes — the prior spec did **not** fully account for AI running coach functionality. Here’s the updated direction.

# RunBuddy — Open-Source AI Running Coach for iOS

## Product vision

RunBuddy is an open-source Swift iOS app that helps users move more consistently, train smarter, and build a sustainable running habit with the help of a friendly AI coach.

The app combines:

* Apple HealthKit activity data
* Strava run history
* Personalized AI coaching
* A playful Buddy mascot
* Transparent, open-source architecture
* CodeYam-powered development workflows and simulations

RunBuddy should feel like a friendly running coach in your pocket: encouraging, practical, adaptive, and never shame-based.

## Strategic goal

RunBuddy is both a useful personal fitness app and a showcase project for CodeYam.

It should demonstrate how CodeYam helps builders create polished, production-quality software by using AI-native development workflows, simulation-first product iteration, and well-scoped code changes.

## Target user

Initial target user: me.

I want to:

* Hit 10K steps per day
* Move regularly throughout the day
* Build running fitness safely
* Avoid injury
* Train for races without overtraining
* Understand my progress from Apple Health and Strava
* Get coaching that feels personal, not generic

Future users:

* Beginner and intermediate runners
* People returning to fitness
* Busy founders/operators who need gentle movement nudges
* Runners who want simple coaching without a full training platform

## Core positioning

RunBuddy is not Strava, Garmin Connect, Nike Run Club, or TrainingPeaks.

It is a friendly, open-source AI running coach focused on:

* Daily movement consistency
* Sustainable running habits
* Injury-aware coaching
* Personalized encouragement
* Clear next actions

## MVP feature set

### 1. Today dashboard

The home screen shows:

* Current step count
* Progress toward daily step goal
* Time since last movement
* Active minutes
* Latest run/walk
* Weekly training load snapshot
* Buddy mood
* AI coach recommendation

Example recommendation:

> You’re at 6,400 steps and haven’t moved in 92 minutes. A 12-minute walk would get you close to your next milestone without adding meaningful training stress.

### 2. HealthKit integration

HealthKit is the source of truth for iOS activity data.

Read:

* Step count
* Walking/running distance
* Workouts
* Active energy
* Heart rate, optional
* Resting heart rate, optional
* Sleep, optional later
* Running route data, later

Write, later:

* App-recorded workouts
* Coach-generated workout metadata
* Notes/tags, if supported appropriately

### 3. Strava integration

Strava is used for running history and social/activity context.

MVP:

* OAuth connection
* Import recent activities
* Show Strava runs/walks/rides
* Deduplicate against HealthKit workouts
* Use Strava data in AI coaching context

Later:

* Upload RunBuddy-recorded activities to Strava
* Add private coach notes
* Detect route preferences
* Compare effort trends over time

### 4. AI running coach

The AI coach should provide practical, personalized, injury-aware guidance.

MVP coach capabilities:

* Daily movement recommendation
* Weekly running plan suggestion
* Run/walk/rest day recommendation
* Race goal planning
* Post-run reflection
* Simple injury-risk flags
* Training consistency feedback
* Encouraging “what should I do today?” answer

Example prompts the user can ask:

* “What should I do today?”
* “Can I run or should I rest?”
* “How do I get to 10K steps without overdoing it?”
* “Am I increasing mileage too fast?”
* “Make me a plan for my October trail half.”
* “I feel tired today — what’s a good low-risk option?”

### 5. Coach context engine

The AI coach should not be a generic chatbot. It should receive structured context.

Inputs:

* Last 7/14/30 days of steps
* Weekly mileage
* Recent workouts
* Run frequency
* Longest recent run
* Recent rest days
* Streaks
* User goals
* Upcoming races
* User notes: soreness, energy, stress, sleep
* Optional Strava effort data

Example structured context:

```json
{
  "dailySteps": 6420,
  "goalSteps": 10000,
  "daysRunThisWeek": 2,
  "weeklyMileage": 12.4,
  "lastRun": {
    "distanceMiles": 4.2,
    "pace": "10:15/mi",
    "date": "2026-06-21"
  },
  "upcomingGoal": "October trail half marathon",
  "userPreference": "injury-avoidant, sustainable progress"
}
```

### 6. Coaching safety rules

The coach should be conservative and practical.

Rules:

* Do not diagnose injuries.
* Encourage medical care for sharp, persistent, worsening, or unusual pain.
* Avoid aggressive mileage jumps.
* Prefer rest, walking, mobility, or easy effort when data suggests fatigue.
* Explain uncertainty.
* Separate fitness coaching from medical advice.
* Use user goals, but never push through warning signs.

### 7. Movement reminders

Smart nudges based on context:

* Inactivity
* Step gap
* Time of day
* Recent training load
* Calendar-aware later
* Weather-aware later

Examples:

* “A 9-minute walk gets you back on track.”
* “You ran yesterday, so Buddy suggests an easy walk instead of another hard effort.”
* “You’re 2,100 steps away. One podcast lap?”

### 8. Buddy mascot layer

Buddy makes the app delightful.

Buddy states:

* Resting
* Ready
* Jogging
* Cheering
* Concerned
* Celebrating
* Recovery mode

Buddy should visually reflect:

* Step progress
* Streaks
* Coach recommendation
* Recovery needs
* Race countdown

## CodeYam showcase layer

RunBuddy should be intentionally built as a public CodeYam showcase.

### What it should demonstrate

* AI-native product development
* Swift app architecture
* HealthKit + Strava integration
* LLM-powered coaching workflows
* Simulation-first feature iteration
* Clean contributor-friendly codebase
* Automated scenario testing
* Transparent product specs and decision logs

### CodeYam-specific showcase ideas

Include a `/docs/codeyam/` folder with:

* Product spec
* AI development workflow notes
* Simulation scenarios
* Prompt logs or sanitized examples
* Feature decision records
* Before/after examples of CodeYam-assisted changes
* Architecture walkthrough

Example docs:

```text
docs/codeyam/
  product-spec.md
  simulation-scenarios.md
  ai-coach-context.md
  development-workflow.md
  decision-log.md
  demo-script.md
```

### Simulation scenarios

Use RunBuddy to show how CodeYam can test user flows before shipping.

Example scenarios:

1. User opens app at 8 pm with 3,200 steps.
2. User ran hard yesterday and asks if they should run today.
3. User is 800 steps from goal at 9:30 pm.
4. User has increased mileage 40% week over week.
5. User connects Strava but denies HealthKit permissions.
6. User has no activity history yet.
7. User is training for a trail half marathon.
8. User reports knee pain after a run.

Each scenario should define:

* User state
* App data
* Expected coach response
* UI state
* Edge cases
* Safety considerations

## Technical architecture

### iOS app

* Swift
* SwiftUI
* HealthKit
* CoreLocation
* UserNotifications
* SwiftData or SQLite
* Swift Charts
* URLSession
* ASWebAuthenticationSession

### AI coach service

For open-source friendliness, support multiple modes:

1. Local mock coach for development
2. Bring-your-own API key mode
3. Optional hosted coach backend
4. Future local model mode, if practical

### Backend

Needed primarily for:

* Strava OAuth token exchange
* Optional AI coach endpoint
* Optional privacy-preserving analytics
* Optional demo mode

Suggested backend:

* TypeScript
* Hono, Express, or Next.js API routes
* SQLite/Postgres optional
* Minimal user accounts, ideally none for MVP

## Open-source repo structure

```text
RunBuddy/
  apps/
    ios/
      RunBuddyApp/
        App/
        Features/
          Onboarding/
          Today/
          Coach/
          ActivityHistory/
          Settings/
        Services/
          HealthKit/
          Strava/
          Notifications/
          Coach/
          Persistence/
        DesignSystem/
        Buddy/
  services/
    strava-oauth/
    coach-api/
  docs/
    product-spec.md
    privacy.md
    contributing.md
    healthkit-setup.md
    strava-setup.md
    codeyam/
      simulation-scenarios.md
      ai-coach-context.md
      development-workflow.md
      decision-log.md
      demo-script.md
  README.md
  LICENSE
```

## AI coach UX

The coach should appear in three places:

### 1. Today card

One clear recommendation.

> Today: easy movement day. You ran yesterday, so Buddy suggests 30–45 minutes of walking and light mobility.

### 2. Ask Coach

Chat-style interface for fitness questions.

### 3. Weekly review

A generated summary:

* What went well
* What changed
* Training risk
* Suggested next week
* One focus area

## Privacy principles

RunBuddy should be privacy-forward by design.

* HealthKit data stays local by default.
* Strava connection is optional.
* AI coach should use the minimum necessary context.
* Users can inspect what data is sent to the coach.
* No hidden analytics.
* No account required for MVP.
* Clear delete/export controls.

## MVP milestones

### Milestone 1: HealthKit movement dashboard

* Onboarding
* HealthKit permissions
* Step dashboard
* Buddy states
* 10K goal progress

### Milestone 2: AI coach mock mode

* Structured local coach context
* Hardcoded/mock coaching responses
* Coach card on Today screen
* Ask Coach screen

### Milestone 3: Real AI coach

* BYO API key or local dev config
* Coach response generation
* Safety rules
* Context preview

### Milestone 4: Activity history

* HealthKit workouts
* Weekly mileage
* Run frequency
* Training load basics

### Milestone 5: Strava import

* OAuth flow
* Import activities
* Deduplication
* Strava activity detail

### Milestone 6: CodeYam showcase polish

* Public README
* Demo video
* Simulation docs
* Architecture docs
* Contributor guide
* Good-first-issue backlog

## Good first open-source issues

* Add a new Buddy mood
* Improve empty states
* Add sample coaching scenario
* Add HealthKit permission explainer copy
* Add weekly summary UI
* Add Strava setup docs
* Add unit tests for step goal logic
* Add mock activity fixtures
* Add accessibility labels
* Improve README screenshots

## Demo narrative

RunBuddy should make a great CodeYam demo because it has:

* Real user data integrations
* Complex permission flows
* AI behavior that needs testing
* Safety-sensitive recommendations
* A delightful consumer UX layer
* Meaningful edge cases
* Strong open-source contributor appeal

Demo storyline:

> We used CodeYam to build and iterate on an open-source Swift fitness app with HealthKit, Strava, and an AI running coach. CodeYam helped us simulate real user states, test coaching logic, catch confusing flows, and refine the product before shipping.

## Success metrics

Personal MVP success:

* I open it daily.
* It helps me hit 10K steps more consistently.
* It gives me useful run/rest guidance.
* It helps me avoid overtraining.
* I can use it as a real training companion.

OSS success:

* Clear README
* Easy local setup
* Contributors can understand the architecture
* At least 10 good-first issues
* Demo scenarios are compelling
* Project clearly showcases CodeYam

Product success:

* Users describe it as friendly, useful, and less intimidating than other running apps.
* The AI coach gives practical recommendations.
* HealthKit and Strava integrations feel reliable.
* The Buddy mascot makes the app memorable.

The biggest product shift: **RunBuddy becomes both a real personal AI coach and a public CodeYam proof-of-work project** — not just a cute step tracker.
